The **Grammy Award for Album of the Year** is an award presented by the [National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") of the United States to "honor artistic achievement, technical proficiency and overall excellence in the recording industry, without regard to [album](/wiki/Album "Album") sales, chart position, or critical reception."[[1]](#cite_note-Overview-1) Album of the Year is the most prestigious category at the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") and is one of the general field categories that have been presented annually since the [1st Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/1st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "1st Annual Grammy Awards") in 1959 alongside [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year"), and [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year").
Credit rules
------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Credit rules")]
Over the years, the rules on who was presented with an award have changed:
* 1959–1965: Artist only.
* 1966–1998: Artist and producer.
* 1999–2002: Artist, producer, and recording engineer or mixer.
* 2003–2017: Artist, featured artist, producer, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer.
* 2018–2020: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (only those who were credited on at least 33% playing time of the album)
* 2021–2023: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (regardless of credited playing time)
* 2024–future: Artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter (of new material), mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer (only those who were credited on at least 20% playing time of the album)
The category expanded to include eight nominees in [2019](/wiki/61st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "61st Annual Grammy Awards")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and ten in [2022](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "64th Annual Grammy Awards").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Beginning with the [2024 ceremony](/wiki/66th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "66th Annual Grammy Awards"), the number of nominees has been reduced back to eight.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)
Album of the Year is awarded for a whole album, and the award is presented to the artist, featured artist, producer, songwriter, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer with significant contributions to that album. The similarly titled Record of the Year is awarded for a single or for one track from an album. This award goes to the artist, producer, mastering engineer, and recording engineer or mixer for that song.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Achievements
------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Achievements")]
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png/250px-191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png)](/wiki/File:191125_Taylor_Swift_at_the_2019_American_Music_Awards.png)
Four-time winner [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift"). She is the first woman to win the award two and three times and the only main credited artist to win four times. She won in [2010](/wiki/52nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "52nd Annual Grammy Awards"), [2016](/wiki/58th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "58th Annual Grammy Awards"), [2021](/wiki/63rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "63rd Annual Grammy Awards") and [2024](/wiki/66th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "66th Annual Grammy Awards"). She is the most nominated female act with 7 nominations.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Frank_Sinatra_%281957_studio_portrait_close-up%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_%281957_studio_portrait_close-up%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_(1957_studio_portrait_close-up).jpg)
[Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") was the first two-time winner and three-time winner. He won in [1960](/wiki/2nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "2nd Annual Grammy Awards"), [1966](/wiki/8th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "8th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1967](/wiki/9th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "9th Annual Grammy Awards"). He is the most nominated artist in this category with 8 nominations.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG/250px-Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stevie_Wonder_1973.JPG)
Three-time winner [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder") won in [1974](/wiki/16th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "16th Annual Grammy Awards"), [1975](/wiki/17th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "17th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1977](/wiki/19th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "19th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg/250px-Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Simon_in_1982.jpg)
Three-time winner [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") won twice as the main credited artist, in [1976](/wiki/18th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "18th Annual Grammy Awards") and [1987](/wiki/29th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "29th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg/250px-U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg)](/wiki/File:U2_on_Joshua_Tree_Tour_2017_Brussels_8-1-17.jpg)
[U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") is the only group act to win twice, in [1988](/wiki/30th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "30th Annual Grammy Awards") and [2006](/wiki/48th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "48th Annual Grammy Awards").
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Adele_-_Live_2016%2C_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg/250px-Adele_-_Live_2016%2C_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adele_-_Live_2016,_Glasgow_SSE_Hydro_03.jpg)
Two-time winner [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") won in [2012](/wiki/54th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "54th Annual Grammy Awards") and [2017](/wiki/59th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "59th Annual Grammy Awards").
[Serban Ghenea](/wiki/Serban_Ghenea "Serban Ghenea") (as engineer/mixer) is the most frequent winner in this category with five awards. [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") (as performer); [John Hanes](/wiki/John_Hanes_(audio_engineer) "John Hanes (audio engineer)") (as engineer/mixer); and [Tom Coyne](/wiki/Tom_Coyne_(music_engineer) "Tom Coyne (music engineer)") and [Randy Merrill](/wiki/Randy_Merrill "Randy Merrill") (as mastering engineers) have won the award four times.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are followed by [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), and [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") (as performers); [Jack Antonoff](/wiki/Jack_Antonoff "Jack Antonoff"), [David Foster](/wiki/David_Foster "David Foster"), [Daniel Lanois](/wiki/Daniel_Lanois "Daniel Lanois"), [Phil Ramone](/wiki/Phil_Ramone "Phil Ramone"), and [Ryan Tedder](/wiki/Ryan_Tedder "Ryan Tedder") (as album producers); [Tom Elmhirst](/wiki/Tom_Elmhirst "Tom Elmhirst") and [Mike Piersante](/wiki/Mike_Piersante "Mike Piersante") and Laura Sisk (as engineers/mixers); and [Bob Ludwig](/wiki/Bob_Ludwig "Bob Ludwig") (as mastering engineer) with three victories each. Coyne, Ghenea, Hanes, and Ludwig are the only people to win the award in three consecutive years. [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") leads all performers with nine nominations: five as a member of [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), three for solo albums, and one as a member of [Wings](/wiki/Wings_(band) "Wings (band)"). McCartney's former bandmate [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") has a total of eight nominations: five as a member of The Beatles, one for a solo album, one for a live album with friends, and one as a member of the [Traveling Wilburys](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys "Traveling Wilburys"). Sinatra leads solo performers with eight nominations: seven for solo albums and one for a duet album. [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") has the most nominations amongst female artists with seven, followed by [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand") with six.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
The first woman to win the award was [Judy Garland](/wiki/Judy_Garland "Judy Garland") in 1962, for *[Judy at Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Judy_at_Carnegie_Hall "Judy at Carnegie Hall")*. Taylor Swift is the first solo female artist to win the award two, three, and four times. [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") has won twice as main credited artist, and [Lauryn Hill](/wiki/Lauryn_Hill "Lauryn Hill"), [Norah Jones](/wiki/Norah_Jones "Norah Jones"), and [Alison Krauss](/wiki/Alison_Krauss "Alison Krauss") also won two times each, first as lead artists for their respective albums, *[The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill](/wiki/The_Miseducation_of_Lauryn_Hill "The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill")*, *[Come Away with Me](/wiki/Come_Away_with_Me "Come Away with Me")*, and *[Raising Sand](/wiki/Raising_Sand "Raising Sand")* (Krauss' collaboration album with [Robert Plant](/wiki/Robert_Plant "Robert Plant")); Hill won her second Album of the Year as a producer of her collaboration on [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)")'s *[Supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural_(Santana_album) "Supernatural (Santana album)")* (featured artists on non-soundtrack albums did not share in the award before 2008), while Jones won again as a featured artist on [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock")'s *[River: The Joni Letters](/wiki/River:_The_Joni_Letters "River: The Joni Letters")*, and Krauss won again having been a featured artist on the *[O Brother, Where Art Thou? – Soundtrack](/wiki/O_Brother,_Where_Art_Thou%3F_(soundtrack) "O Brother, Where Art Thou? (soundtrack)")*.
[Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish") is the youngest main credit artist to win in the category, winning for her debut album *When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?* in 2020 at the age of 18. [The Peasall Sisters](/wiki/The_Peasall_Sisters "The Peasall Sisters"), Sarah, Hannah, and Leah, are the category's youngest credited winners, winning for their contributions to the *O Brother, Where Art Thou? – Soundtrack* at ages 13, 9, and 7 respectively. Leah Peasall is the youngest winner of any Grammy in any category.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The youngest person to make an appearance on an Album of the Year is Stevie Wonder's daughter Aisha Morris, who appeared as an infant for "[Isn't She Lovely?](/wiki/Isn%27t_She_Lovely%3F "Isn't She Lovely?")" on the album *[Songs in the Key of Life](/wiki/Songs_in_the_Key_of_Life "Songs in the Key of Life")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
[Christopher Cross](/wiki/Christopher_Cross "Christopher Cross") and Billie Eilish are the only artists to receive Grammys for Album of the Year as well as [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year"), [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year"), and [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), each winning all four "general field" categories in a single ceremony year. Adele was the first artist to win awards for Album of the Year, Record of the Year, Song of the Year, and Best New Artist on separate occasions. Five artists have won both Album of the Year and Best New Artist in the same year: [Bob Newhart](/wiki/Bob_Newhart "Bob Newhart") (*[The Button-Down Mind of Bob Newhart](/wiki/The_Button-Down_Mind_of_Bob_Newhart "The Button-Down Mind of Bob Newhart")* in 1961), Lauryn Hill (*The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill* in 1999), and Norah Jones (*Come Away with Me* in 2003) along with Christopher Cross (*[Christopher Cross](/wiki/Christopher_Cross_(album) "Christopher Cross (album)")* in 1981) and Billie Eilish (*When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?* in 2020) mentioned above.
Frank Sinatra's *[Come Dance with Me!](/wiki/Come_Dance_with_Me!_(album) "Come Dance with Me! (album)")* was the first album by a [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") artist to win, [

This is the [latest accepted revision](/wiki/Wikipedia:Pending_changes "Wikipedia:Pending changes"), [reviewed](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Special:Log&type=review&page=List_of_NBA_single-game_scoring_leaders) on *6 April 2026*.
This is a complete listing of [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") players who have scored 60 or more [points](/wiki/Point_(basketball) "Point (basketball)") in a regular season game.
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Wilt_Chamberlain_100_Point_Game_1962_%28original%29.jpg/250px-Wilt_Chamberlain_100_Point_Game_1962_%28original%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilt_Chamberlain_100_Point_Game_1962_(original).jpg)
[Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain") scored [100 points in 1962, which is the current NBA record](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain%27s_100-point_game "Wilt Chamberlain's 100-point game").
This feat has been accomplished 93 times in NBA history by 38 different players, while just eleven players have joined the 70-point club, with three players having scored at least 80 points. Only 10 players have scored 60 or more points on more than one occasion: [Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain") (32 times); [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant") (6 times); [Damian Lillard](/wiki/Damian_Lillard "Damian Lillard") (5 times); [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") and [James Harden](/wiki/James_Harden "James Harden") (4 times); [Elgin Baylor](/wiki/Elgin_Baylor "Elgin Baylor") and [Luka Dončić](/wiki/Luka_Don%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Luka Dončić") (3 times); and [Karl-Anthony Towns](/wiki/Karl-Anthony_Towns "Karl-Anthony Towns"), [Devin Booker](/wiki/Devin_Booker "Devin Booker"), and [Stephen Curry](/wiki/Stephen_Curry "Stephen Curry") (2 times). Chamberlain holds the single-game scoring record, having [scored 100 points in a game in 1962](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain%27s_100-point_game "Wilt Chamberlain's 100-point game"). The youngest player to score at least 60 points in a regular season game is [Devin Booker](/wiki/Devin_Booker "Devin Booker") (70 points; 20 years and 145 days),[[1]](#cite_note-1) and the oldest is Bryant (60 points; 37 years and 234 days).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
[![A screencap of the title card from the trailer of Gone with the Wind.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/250px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg)
*[Gone with the Wind](/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film) "Gone with the Wind (film)")* held the record for the highest-grossing film for twenty-five years and, when adjusted for inflation, has earned more than any other film
[Films](/wiki/Film "Film") generate income from several revenue streams, including [theatrical exhibition](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater"), [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video"), [television broadcast rights](/wiki/Broadcasting_rights "Broadcasting rights"), and [merchandising](/wiki/Merchandising "Merchandising"). However, theatrical [box-office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") earnings are the primary metric for trade publications in assessing the success of a film, mostly because of the availability of the data compared to sales figures for home video and broadcast rights, but also because of historical practice. Included on the list are charts of the top box-office earners (ranked by both the [nominal and real value](/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics) "Real versus nominal value (economics)") of their revenue), a chart of high-grossing films by calendar year, a timeline showing the transition of the highest-grossing film record, and a chart of the highest-grossing film franchises and series. All charts are ranked by international theatrical box-office performance where possible, excluding income derived from home video, broadcasting rights, and merchandise.
Traditionally, [war films](/wiki/War_film "War film"), [musicals](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film"), and [historical dramas](/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films "List of historical drama films") have been the most popular genres, but [franchise films](/wiki/Film_franchise "Film franchise") have been among the best performers of the 21st century. There is strong interest in the [superhero genre](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film"), with eleven films in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") featuring among the nominal top-earners. The most successful superhero film, *[Avengers: Endgame](/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame "Avengers: Endgame")*, is also the second-highest-grossing film on the nominal earnings chart, and there are four films in total based on the *[Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(comics) "Avengers (comics)")* comic books charting in the top twenty. Other [Marvel Comics adaptations](/wiki/List_of_films_based_on_Marvel_Comics_publications "List of films based on Marvel Comics publications") have also had success with the *[Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film "Spider-Man in film")* and *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)")* properties, while films based on *[Batman](/wiki/Batman_in_film "Batman in film")* and *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_in_film "Superman in film")* from [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") have generally performed well. Beyond the superhero genre, *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* is the most represented franchise in the nominal earnings chart with five films. Although the nominal earnings chart is dominated by films adapted from pre-existing properties and sequels, it is headed by *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, which is an original work. Animated family films have performed consistently well, with [Disney films](/wiki/List_of_Disney_theatrical_animated_features "List of Disney theatrical animated features") enjoying lucrative re-releases prior to the home-video era. Disney also enjoyed later success with films such as *[Frozen](/wiki/Frozen_(2013_film) "Frozen (2013 film)")* and [its sequel](/wiki/Frozen_2 "Frozen 2"), *[Zootopia](/wiki/Zootopia "Zootopia")* and [its sequel](/wiki/Zootopia_2 "Zootopia 2"), and *[The Lion King](/wiki/The_Lion_King "The Lion King")* (along with [its computer-animated remake](/wiki/The_Lion_King_(2019_film) "The Lion King (2019 film)")), as well as its [Pixar](/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films "List of Pixar films") division, of which *[Inside Out 2](/wiki/Inside_Out_2 "Inside Out 2")*, *[Incredibles 2](/wiki/Incredibles_2 "Incredibles 2")*, and *[Toy Story 3](/wiki/Toy_Story_3 "Toy Story 3")* and *[4](/wiki/Toy_Story_4 "Toy Story 4")* have been the best performers. Beyond Disney and Pixar animation, China's *[Ne Zha 2](/wiki/Ne_Zha_2 "Ne Zha 2")* (the [highest-grossing animated film](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_animated_films "List of highest-grossing animated films")), and the *[Despicable Me](/wiki/Despicable_Me "Despicable Me")* and *[Shrek](/wiki/Shrek_(franchise) "Shrek (franchise)")* series have met with the most success.
While [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") has eroded the achievements of most films from the 1960s and 1970s, there are franchises originating from that period that are still active. Besides the *Star Wars* franchise, *[James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_in_film "James Bond in film")* films are still being released periodically; both are among the highest-grossing franchises. Some of the older films that held the record of highest-grossing film still have respectable grosses by today's standards, but no longer compete numerically against today's top-earners in an era of much higher individual ticket prices. When those prices are adjusted for inflation, however, then *[Gone with the Wind](/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film) "Gone with the Wind (film)")*—which was the highest-grossing film outright for twenty-five years—is still the highest-grossing film of all time. All grosses on the list are expressed in [U.S. dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") at their nominal value, except where stated otherwise.

Highest-grossing films
----------------------
[![A portrait of a middle aged man with greying hair.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Cameron_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)
Three of the four highest-grossing films, including *Avatar* at the top, were written and directed by [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron").
With a worldwide box-office gross of over $2.9 billion, *Avatar* is proclaimed to be the "highest-grossing" film, but such claims usually refer to [theatrical](/wiki/Movie_theatre "Movie theatre") revenues only and do not take into account [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video") and [television](/wiki/Television "Television") income, which can form a significant portion of a film's earnings. Once revenue from home entertainment is factored in, it is not immediately clear which film is the most successful. *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")* earned $1.2 billion from [video](/wiki/Video "Video") and [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") sales

**John Clarke** (born 10 February 1942) is a British [experimental physicist](/wiki/Experimental_physicist "Experimental physicist") and Professor Emeritus at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is known for his various works on measurement devices based on [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity"). [Steven Girvin](/wiki/Steven_Girvin "Steven Girvin") has called Clarke "the godfather of superconducting electronics".[[2]](#cite_note-2)
In the 1980s, Clarke led a research team that included [John M. Martinis](/wiki/John_M._Martinis "John M. Martinis") and [Michel Devoret](/wiki/Michel_Devoret "Michel Devoret").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Their discoveries in [macroscopic quantum phenomena](/wiki/Macroscopic_quantum_phenomena "Macroscopic quantum phenomena") using the [Josephson effect](/wiki/Josephson_effect "Josephson effect") earned them the [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/2025_Nobel_Prizes "2025 Nobel Prizes") in 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)
Education and career
--------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Clarke_(physicist)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Education and career")]
John Clarke was born on 10 February 1942 in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England.[[4]](#cite_note-Nobel-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He attended the [Perse School](/wiki/The_Perse_School "The Perse School"), before embarking on a [Natural Sciences](/wiki/Natural_Science_Tripos "Natural Science Tripos") degree at [Christ's College](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Cambridge "Christ's College, Cambridge"), [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge").[[6]](#cite_note-timesnownews-6) He graduated with a [B.A.](/wiki/B.A. "B.A.") in Physics in 1964, and then studied for a [Ph.D.](/wiki/Ph.D. "Ph.D.") in Physics in the [Royal Society Mond Laboratory](/wiki/New_Museums_Site "New Museums Site") at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge").[[7]](#cite_note-berkeley-7)
In 1965, Clarke became one of the first students to enter the newly founded [Darwin College, Cambridge](/wiki/Darwin_College,_Cambridge "Darwin College, Cambridge"), and was the first president of the Darwin College students' association.[[8]](#cite_note-8) While conducting his doctoral work—which was supervised by [Brian Pippard](/wiki/Brian_Pippard "Brian Pippard")—Clarke developed a very sensitive [voltmeter](/wiki/Voltmeter "Voltmeter"), which he later called "SLUG" (Superconducting Low-inductance Undulatory Galvanometer).[[6]](#cite_note-timesnownews-6)[[7]](#cite_note-berkeley-7) He obtained his doctorate in 1968.[[7]](#cite_note-berkeley-7) Clarke has said at various times that his work was influenced by Nobel laureate [Brian Josephson](/wiki/Brian_Josephson "Brian Josephson"), who predicted the [Josephson effect](/wiki/Josephson_effect "Josephson effect") in 1962 and was also a previous student of Pippard.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
After completing his doctorate, Clarke gained a postdoctoral research position at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), and subsequently worked at Berkeley for his whole academic career, as Assistant Professor (1969), Associate Professor (1971), and as Professor of Physics (1973–2010).[[11]](#cite_note-Christs-11) In 1969, Clarke also joined [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory"), eventually retiring as a faculty senior scientist in the Materials Sciences Division in 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-LBNL-12)
Clarke's association with the University of Cambridge continued, after he moved to the United States.[[11]](#cite_note-Christs-11) In 1972, he was elected a Fellow of Christ's College; in 1989, he was a visiting fellow at [Clare Hall, Cambridge](/wiki/Clare_Hall,_Cambridge "Clare Hall, Cambridge"), and in 1998 was elected a by-fellow of [Churchill College, Cambridge](/wiki/Churchill_College,_Cambridge "Churchill College, Cambridge").[[11]](#cite_note-Christs-11) Clarke was awarded a [D.Sc.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Science "Doctor of Science") from the University of Cambridge in 2003.[[11]](#cite_note-Christs-11) He was elected an Honorary Fellow of Christ's College in 1997, and of Darwin College in 2023.[[11]](#cite_note-Christs-11)
Research
--------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Clarke_(physicist)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Research")]
Clarke's research focuses on [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity") and superconducting electronics, particularly in the development and application of [superconducting quantum interference devices](/wiki/SQUID "SQUID") (SQUIDs), which are ultrasensitive detectors of [magnetic flux](/wiki/Magnetic_flux "Magnetic flux").[[13]](#cite_note-RS-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Ford2005-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Lakhani2025-15)
In 1985, Clarke, [John M. Martinis](/wiki/John_M._Martinis "John M. Martinis") (his Ph.D. student), and [Michel Devoret](/wiki/Michel_Devoret "Michel Devoret") (a postdoctoral researcher at the time) demonstrated the quantum behaviour of a [Josephson junction](/wiki/Josephson_effect "Josephson effect").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16) They showed that at low temperature, a macroscopic electronic state associated with superconductors underwent [quantum tunnelling](/wiki/Quantum_tunnelling "Quantum tunnelling") at zero voltage.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The same year, by sending [microwave](/wiki/Microwave "Microwave") pulses of the system, the [resonances showed quantised energy levels](/wiki/Phase_qubit#Quantized_energy_levels "Phase qubit").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This experiment was the first evidence of [circuit quantum electrodynamics](/wiki/Circuit_quantum_electrodynamics "Circuit quantum electrodynamics"), that would become later the basis for [superconducting quantum computing](/wiki/Superconducting_quantum_computing "Superconducting quantum computing").[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The work, which was recognized with the [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") in 2025, was largely funded by the Office of Basic Energy Sciences in the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy").
[[21]](#cite_note-APS-21)
Clarke has also worked in the application of SQUIDs configured as quantum-noise limited amplifiers to search for the [axion](/wiki/Axion "Axion"), a possible component of [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter").[[13]](#cite_note-RS-13)
Awards and honours
------------------
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Clarke_(physicist)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Awards and honours")]
Clarke obtained an [Alfred P. Sloan](/wiki/Alfred_P._Sloan "Alfred P. Sloan") fellowship (1970)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and a [Guggenheim Fellowship](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellowship "Guggenheim Fellowship") (1977).[[23]](#cite_note-23) Clarke was elected a [Fellow of the Royal Society](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") in 1986.[[13]](#cite_note-RS-13) He was awarded the [Joseph F. Keithley Award For Advances in Measurement Science](/wiki/Joseph_F._Keithley_Award "Joseph F. Keithley Award") in 1998,[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Comstock Prize in Physics](/wiki/Comstock_Prize_in_Physics "Comstock Prize in Physics") in 1999,[[25]](#cite_note-Comstock-25) the [Hughes Medal](/wiki/Hughes_Medal "Hughes Medal")[[13]](#cite_note-RS-13) and the Olli V. Lounasmaa Memorial Prize in 2004.[[26]](#cite_note-26) He was elected a Foreign Associate of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") in May 2012.[[27]](#cite_note-27) He was elected to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") in 2017.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
In 2021, the [Micius Quantum Prize](/wiki/Micius_Quantum_Prize "Micius Quantum Prize") was jointly awarded to Clarke, [Michel Devoret](/wiki/Michel_Devoret "Michel Devoret") and [Yasunobu Nakamura](/wiki/Yasunobu_Nakamura "Yasunobu Nakamura").[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29)
